attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of burning pain in his hands and feet, which worsens with exercise and hot weather. He reports reduced sweating and multiple small, dark red spots that have appeared on his trunk and thighs. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His maternal uncle died at age 45 from kidney failure. He denies excessive sun exposure, and he drinks 3 standard alcoholic drinks five times per week since finishing school.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple 2-3 mm red-purple papules clustered on his trunk and upper thighs. His peripheral pulses are normal, and neurological examination reveals no focal deficits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of symptoms suggesting Fabry disease: acroparaesthesia (burning pain in extremities), hypohidrosis (reduced sweating), and angiokeratomas (small red-purple papules). The X-linked inheritance pattern is suggested by the history of kidney failure in a maternal uncle, as males are more severely affected, and untreated disease can lead to renal failure, arrythmia, stroke and cardiac failure. Patients may also present with corneal clouding (cornea verticillata), left ventricular hypertrophy and hearing loss.

Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry disease, angiokeratoma corporis diffusum or Anderson-Fabry disease) is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficient activity of alpha-galactosidase A. It may be as common as 1:40,000 births in European populations however diagnosis for atypical cases is more difficult thus the prevalence is not clearly known. This leads to accumulation of glycosphingolipids in various tissues, resulting in progressive organ damage affecting the kidneys, heart, and nervous system. Angiokeratomas are non-blanching dilated blood vessels typically on the trunk and thighs, but also on the lips and mouth. Early recognition is crucial as enzyme replacement therapy can prevent or slow organ damage.

Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy with burning sensations in hands and feet, but would not explain the characteristic skin findings. It typically presents with additional features such as macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, and cognitive changes, and can be associated with excessive alcohol intake.

Multiple cherry angiomas are common benign vascular lesions that typically appear in adults over 30. They are bright benign red, dome-shaped papules and are not associated with systemic symptoms or neuropathic pain.

Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia typically presents with recurrent epistaxis and mucocutaneous telangiectasias, particularly on the lips and oral mucosa. The absence of these features and the presence of neuropathic symptoms make this diagnosis unlikely.

Cutaneous vasculitis typically presents with palpable purpura rather than fixed vascular lesions. The lesions are often painful and may ulcerate. The chronic nature of this patient's symptoms and the characteristic appearance of the skin lesions make vasculitis unlikely.
learningPoints: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry disease) typically presents with angiokeratomas, neuropathic pain, and reduced sweating in young men.
Early recognition of Fabry disease is important as enzyme replacement therapy can prevent progression of organ damage.
references: Jin Q, Oakley A, Mitchell G, McGivern M. Fabry Disease. DermNet NZ. 2019. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/fabry-disease

Kok K, Zwiers KC, Boot RG, Overkleeft HS, Aerts JMFG, Artola M. Fabry Disease: Molecular Basis, Pathophysiology, Diagnostics and Potential Therapeutic Directions. Biomolecules. 2021; 11(2):271.  Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2218-273X/11/2/271
Besekar SM, Jogdand SD, Naqvi WM. Fabry Disease and Its Management: A Literature Analysis. Cureus. 2023.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10154914/
